proposals.title: A Multi-Fidelity Distillation Framework: Integrating Variational Information Distillation with Activation Matching for Robust Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Model compression via knowledge distillation is crucial for deploying complex models in resource-constrained clinical and embedded systems, a key theme in Lawrence's work on biomarkers and patient data. Pre-2026, standard distillation losses like Kullback-Leibler divergence on outputs or mean-squared error on intermediate features often fail to capture the full probabilistic structure and multi-scale information of the teacher model, limiting fidelity and robustness in the compressed student. A principled, unified framework is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining the Variational Information Distillation (VID) objective, which maximizes mutual information between teacher and student representations, with established activation matching losses will create a more effective, multi-fidelity distillation framework that preserves richer statistical dependencies and improves downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a software framework extending the Emukit philosophy to model compression. The core method involves a composite loss function: L_total = λ_vid * L_vid + λ_act * L_act. L_vid will be implemented via a variational bound on mutual information, using a learned critic network. L_act will incorporate multi-layer feature matching, potentially inspired by attention transfer or neuron selectivity transfer. We will apply this framework to compress deep models on biomarker datasets (e.g., longitudinal PIMS-TS data) and simulation tasks, treating the teacher-student relationship as a multi-fidelity system to be actively designed and evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, flexible distillation framework grounded in information theory and multi-fidelity modeling, 2) open-source software integrating this framework into the Emukit ecosystem for decision making under uncertainty, and 3) empirical validation on clinical and scientific datasets demonstrating improved robustness and data efficiency in distilled models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of the information-theoretic VID objective with geometric feature matching losses within a unified probabilistic framework, moving beyond simple linear combinations to a principled, multi-objective formulation for capturing different 'fidelities' of knowledge from the teacher model.

proposals.title: Variational Information Distillation for Biomarker Discovery in Pediatric Populations with Scarce Data
proposals.motivation: In clinical biomarker research, especially for rare pediatric conditions like PIMS-TS, datasets are often very small, limiting the power of predictive models. Conversely, large, general biomedical datasets exist but may not directly apply. A method to safely and efficiently transfer knowledge from large, potentially noisy sources to small, specific target datasets is critically needed to enable robust inference where data is most scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: That Variational Information Distillation (VID), a technique for compressing knowledge from one model into another, can be effectively adapted within a multi-fidelity or hierarchical Bayesian framework to transfer robust, generalizable patterns from a large, source dataset to significantly improve predictive accuracy and uncertainty quantification on a very small, related target dataset.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit toolkit to implement a probabilistic distillation framework. A complex model (e.g., deep Gaussian process) will be trained on the large source dataset. Using VID, its predictive distribution will be distilled into a simpler, more data-efficient model (e.g., a sparse GP or Bayesian linear model) suitable for the small target dataset. This process will be guided by a hierarchical prior that formalizes the 'between'-dataset relationship, akin to multilevel modeling used for longitudinal biomarker data, ensuring transfer is conservative and uncertainty-preserving.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel software framework within Emukit for data-efficient decision-making under uncertainty in data-scarce domains. It will deliver a principled methodology for biomarker model personalization or adaptation for rare diseases. The research will also advance understanding of how to design transfer learning systems that respect the fidelity and inherent uncertainty of different data sources.
proposals.novelty: While VID and multi-fidelity modeling exist separately, their integration for knowledge transfer from large-scale observational data to very small clinical datasets is novel. The approach moves beyond simple fine-tuning by using a rigorous information-theoretic objective within a decision-making under uncertainty framework, explicitly designed for the high-stakes, low-data scenarios characteristic of Neil Lawrence's research themes.

proposals.title: A Latent Dynamical Process Model for Non-Stationary Multi-Task Learning with Applications to Longitudinal Biomarker Analysis
proposals.motivation: Multi-task learning (MTL) with Gaussian processes is powerful for leveraging shared structure across related tasks, such as modeling longitudinal biomarker responses in different patient cohorts. However, existing MTL kernels assume stationary, time-invariant task correlations, which is unrealistic in clinical settings where disease progression or treatment effects can fundamentally alter relationships between biomarkers over time. A flexible, data-driven model of evolving task covariance is needed to improve predictive accuracy and interpretability in complex, real-world systems like pediatric inflammatory syndromes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the covariance structure between tasks as a function of an inferred latent stochastic dynamical process will capture non-stationary, evolving inter-task relationships more effectively than static or parametrically constrained multi-task kernels, leading to superior predictive performance in longitudinal clinical datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where observed data is governed by task-specific Gaussian processes. The core novelty is that the cross-task covariance matrix is not fixed but is a function of the state of a latent, time-evolving stochastic process (e.g., a Gaussian process or a state-space model). This latent process's dynamics will be learned via variational inference, potentially using the Emukit framework for decision-making under uncertainty. The model will be implemented in Python, leveraging GPflow or GPyTorch, and evaluated on longitudinal biomarker data from pediatric patients, such as the PIMS-TS dataset, to infer how biomarker relationships shift during treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, more expressive class of non-stationary multi-task Gaussian process models that automatically discover evolving correlation structures from data. It will provide a principled software framework for analyzing longitudinal, multi-outcome clinical trials and complex systems where relationships between variables are dynamic. The method will enhance the interpretability of time-varying interactions in biomedical data.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the decoupling of the task covariance structure from a pre-specified kernel form, instead making it a learnable function of a latent dynamical system. This moves beyond existing non-stationary or deep kernel MTL methods by allowing the inter-task geometry itself to evolve according to a data-adaptive stochastic process.

proposals.title: Dynamic Multi-Task Learning with Non-Stationary Kernels Informed by Data Supply Chain Signals
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning pipelines, especially in clinical and industrial settings, depend on complex, evolving data flows. Current multi-task learning (MTL) methods treat task relationships as static or learned solely from output data, ignoring the rich, real-time signals from the data pipeline itself (e.g., provenance, quality drift, latency). This disconnect makes models brittle to upstream changes in data supply, a critical vulnerability for reliable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That modeling the data pipeline as a logistics network and using its operational metrics (e.g., data lineage, freshness, error rates) to dynamically parameterize the inter-task correlation structure in an MTL model will yield more robust and anticipatory learning, improving performance under non-stationary data flow conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a Gaussian process-based MTL framework where the core multi-output kernel's parameters are governed by a secondary, input-dependent process. Supply chain signals (data latency, version, missingness) will serve as inputs to this process, implemented via deep kernel learning or a Bayesian linear model within the Emukit framework. This creates a non-stationary correlation kernel, `k(t_i, t_j | s_i, s_j)`, where task correlations evolve with supply chain states `s`. We will validate the framework on real-world longitudinal biomarker datasets, treating patient subgroups or time windows as tasks, with pipeline metadata simulated from hospital data logistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unifying framework that bridges data operations and model learning, producing systems that co-adapt. It will extend the Emukit toolkit with new modules for supply-chain-aware uncertainty modeling and deliver a methodological advance in MTL through explicitly dynamic, externally-informed correlation structures.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal treatment of data pipeline operational metrics as first-class, dynamic inputs for shaping core MTL model parameters, moving beyond static kernels or those learned only from task output data. This fusion of supply chain management principles with probabilistic MTL is unexplored.

proposals.title: Adaptive Kernel Composition Networks for Multi-Fidelity Biomarker Modelling in Paediatric Inflammatory Disease
proposals.motivation: In clinical biomarker studies, such as longitudinal analysis of PIMS-TS, data is heterogeneous, comprising continuous biomarker levels, categorical disease states, and ordinal symptom scores. Pre-2026 multi-output Gaussian processes (GPs) rely on pre-defined, static kernel structures (e.g., ICM, LCM) that cannot adapt to the complex, task-specific relationships between these disparate outputs. This limits robust joint inference and the ability to share information effectively across data types, a core challenge in systems medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework that dynamically composes kernels via a data-dependent gating network will learn more expressive and task-appropriate inductive biases for joint modelling of heterogeneous clinical outputs, leading to improved predictive performance and uncertainty quantification compared to static multi-output kernels.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep kernel learning architecture where a neural network gating function, conditioned on inputs, outputs weights for combining a dictionary of base kernels (e.g., RBF, Matern, periodic). This adaptive composite kernel will parameterize a multi-output GP, with outputs structured for mixed types (continuous, categorical via latent Gaussian models). The framework will be implemented within the Emukit toolkit, leveraging its decision-making under uncertainty components. Training will use a variational evidence lower bound, optimized jointly for the GP parameters and the gating network weights.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, principled framework for joint learning on heterogeneous biomedical data, directly addressing the 'between' theme of relating disparate data types. It will extend the software capabilities of Emukit for clinical decision support. The method will be validated on real-world longitudinal paediatric biomarker data, demonstrating improved utility for tasks like patient stratification and treatment response prediction.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the data-driven, adaptive composition of kernels for multi-output learning, moving beyond fixed linear combinations. This integrates the flexibility of neural network conditioning with the principled uncertainty of GPs specifically for heterogeneous outputs, a synthesis not fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Scalable Structured Prediction via Joint Latent Embeddings: A Framework for Biomarker Trajectory and Clinical Event Forecasting
proposals.motivation: Predicting structured outputs like temporal biomarker trajectories or clinical event graphs is crucial for personalized medicine but computationally challenging. Pre-2026 kernel methods for structured prediction become intractable for complex, high-dimensional outputs due to the need for explicit kernel construction over the output space. This limits their application to large-scale, real-world clinical datasets where we need to model relationships between patients, their treatments, and multivariate, longitudinal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning a joint, finite-dimensional latent embedding for input-output pairs with a structured contrastive loss will yield a scalable and effective model for structured prediction, bypassing the intractable kernel matrices of classical approaches while capturing the complex dependencies in clinical data.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep neural network architecture that maps an input (e.g., patient baseline features) and a candidate structured output (e.g., a sequence of biomarker values) into a shared Euclidean embedding space. The model will be trained using a structured hinge or triplet loss, pulling the embedding of the true input-output pair together and pushing it apart from plausible, corrupted outputs. We will leverage and extend the Emukit toolkit's decision-making under uncertainty components for Bayesian experimental design, allowing us to actively query which patient features or time points are most informative for refining the embeddings, particularly for children's biomarker responses as seen in PIMS-TS research.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable software framework for structured prediction applicable to clinical biomarker trajectories and beyond, directly addressing the 'between' systems of patients and outcomes. It will demonstrate how latent space learning integrates with Bayesian design principles for efficient data collection, contributing a novel methodology to Neil Lawrence's research themes of biomarkers, design, and systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of contrastive latent space learning for structured outputs with Bayesian experimental design queries, creating a unified framework for prediction and data acquisition. This moves beyond traditional kernel methods and standard deep learning by explicitly designing the embedding space for structured loss optimization and active learning.

proposals.title: Tensor Network Gaussian Processes: A Scalable Framework for Multi-Output Learning via Kronecker-Structured Covariance Approximations
proposals.motivation: Multi-output Gaussian processes (MOGPs) are essential for modeling correlated functions in spatiotemporal forecasting and multi-task learning, such as predicting multiple biomarkers in longitudinal patient studies. However, their standard formulation scales as O(n³p³), becoming computationally prohibitive for large datasets (n) with many outputs (p). This bottleneck limits their application to modern, high-dimensional problems in healthcare and environmental science where capturing complex dependencies is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: By imposing a Kronecker-structured, tensor network-inspired factorization on the MOGP covariance matrix, we can achieve accurate approximations that reduce computational complexity to near-linear scaling in n and p, while preserving the ability to model rich inter-output correlations.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel MOGP framework where the full covariance matrix is approximated as a Kronecker product of smaller factor matrices across input dimensions and output channels, analogous to a Matrix Product State tensor network. We will adapt variational inference algorithms from the Emukit toolkit to learn these factor matrices efficiently. For model training and prediction, we will exploit the Kronecker structure to use linear algebra solvers that require only O(n p) operations, rather than O(n³p³), and derive theoretical error bounds for the approximation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, open-source software framework for large-scale multi-output regression, directly extending the Emukit philosophy. It will provide a principled connection between tensor network methods from quantum physics and probabilistic machine learning, enabling new applications in domains like multi-fidelity simulation and longitudinal biomarker analysis where handling many correlated outputs is key.
proposals.novelty: While Kronecker methods exist for single-output GPs, their principled integration with tensor network decompositions to factorize both the input and output dimensions of an MOGP is novel. This cross-disciplinary analogy provides a non-obvious pathway to scalability by directly importing solution strategies from quantum many-body physics.

proposals.title: Bayesian Averaging of Multi-Output Kernels via Reversible-Jump MCMC: A Phylogenetic Analogy for Biomarker and Systems Modelling
proposals.motivation: Multi-output Gaussian processes (GPs) are crucial for modelling correlated systems, such as longitudinal biomarker responses in children or multi-fidelity physical simulations, where the choice of kernel structure (e.g., LMC, separable) critically impacts predictions and uncertainty quantification. Current practice relies on ad-hoc selection or maximum marginal likelihood, which ignores model uncertainty and can lead to overconfident inferences. A principled Bayesian framework for kernel selection is needed to robustly propagate structural uncertainty into predictions and decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting Bayesian model averaging techniques from computational phylogenetics, specifically reversible-jump MCMC (rjMCMC), we can develop a coherent sampling framework that jointly infers the multi-output kernel structure, its hyperparameters, and latent functions, leading to more robust predictive distributions that account for uncertainty in the inter-output correlation model.
proposals.method: We will define a discrete prior over a library of multi-output kernel structures (Linear Model of Coregionalization, separable, convolutional). We will implement an rjMCMC sampler within the Emukit framework, designing reversible jump moves that allow transitions between parameterizations of differing dimensionality (e.g., changing the number of latent functions in an LMC). The sampler will jointly sample the kernel index, its hyperparameters, and latent function values, with proposals informed by gradient information from the GP log-likelihood to improve mixing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general-purpose rjMCMC implementation for Bayesian model averaging over multi-output GP kernels, directly quantifying uncertainty in correlation structure. It will deliver a practical software tool within Emukit for decision-making under uncertainty in systems biology and computational physics, moving beyond point estimates of kernel choice.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct translation of the well-established phylogenetic model averaging paradigm—a solution to a similar discrete model choice problem—to the kernel learning domain, creating a rigorous Bayesian alternative to existing heuristic or optimization-based multi-output kernel selection methods.

proposals.title: Bayesian Averaging of Multi-Output Kernels for Robust Longitudinal Biomarker Modeling
proposals.motivation: Multi-output Gaussian processes (MOGPs) are crucial for modeling correlated longitudinal data, such as biomarker trajectories in pediatric patients (e.g., PIMS-TS). However, selecting the correct multi-output kernel structure (e.g., ICM, LCM, CPM) is a critical, non-trivial model design choice that significantly impacts predictions and clinical insights. Current practice relies on point estimates or heuristic selection, lacking a principled framework to quantify uncertainty over kernel parameterizations themselves.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian model selection and averaging framework for multi-output kernels will yield more robust and better calibrated uncertainty estimates in longitudinal clinical prediction tasks, outperforming single-kernel models selected via maximum marginal likelihood.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework within the Emukit software ecosystem, extending its Bayesian optimization and experimental design capabilities. The core method involves placing a discrete prior over a set of candidate multi-output kernel structures (e.g., separable, convolutional, graph-based). We will compute or approximate the model evidence (marginal likelihood) for each kernel using nested sampling or variational inference, enabling posterior model probabilities. Model averaging will be performed by weighting predictions from each kernel by its posterior probability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, uncertainty-aware methodology for kernel design in MOGPs, directly applicable to complex longitudinal biomarker studies. It will deliver open-source software tools within Emukit, enabling researchers to move beyond ad-hoc kernel selection and improve the reliability of predictions in clinical and systems biology contexts.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian model selection is established for single-output kernels, its rigorous application to the structured space of multi-output kernels—where choices encode assumptions about relationships between patients, biomarkers, and time—is novel. This bridges the gap between flexible kernel design and robust, automated model choice.

proposals.title: Deep Hierarchical Kernels: A Multi-Scale Homogenization Approach for Gaussian Processes on Grid Data
proposals.motivation: Gaussian processes (GPs) offer principled uncertainty quantification but struggle with complex, non-stationary patterns in high-dimensional grid-like data, such as medical images or spatial biomarker maps. While deep kernels and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have shown promise, a principled, interpretable framework for designing multi-scale, structured covariance functions remains elusive. This work addresses the gap by drawing a formal analogy to multi-fidelity modeling and hierarchical homogenization in computational materials science, where effective properties are propagated across scales.
proposals.hypothesis: That the mathematical principles of hierarchical homogenization from materials science can be systematically translated to construct deep, multi-output GP kernels, resulting in models that capture complex, multi-scale dependencies while retaining the interpretability and uncertainty calibration of kernel methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a base kernel operates on small, local grid 'patches' (analogous to a representative volume element). Its outputs (covariance features) will be aggregated via a deterministic, differentiable pooling operation (e.g., striding or averaging). This aggregated representation becomes the input domain for the next kernel layer, building a deep compositional structure. The entire hierarchy will be implemented within the Emukit framework, with parameters learned via gradient-based optimization of the marginal likelihood, enabling automatic relevance determination across scales.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a new, interpretable class of deep kernels for GPs that explicitly encode multi-scale inductive biases, improving performance on tasks like spatial biomarker mapping and image-based regression. It will also create a novel theoretical bridge between computational homogenization and kernel design, enriching both fields.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct translation of the homogenization concept—solving at a fine scale to inform a coarser model—into a learnable kernel architecture, moving beyond simple additive compositions or neural network embeddings used in pre-2026 deep kernel methods.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Context-Conditional Dependency Structures for Multi-Output Gaussian Processes in Biomarker Trajectory Modelling
proposals.motivation: In multi-output regression tasks, such as modelling longitudinal biomarker panels in paediatric inflammatory syndromes (PIMS-TS), the complex interdependencies between outputs are often context-dependent, varying with patient features or disease stage. Standard multi-output kernels assume a fixed, global dependency structure, limiting their expressiveness for heterogeneous real-world data. A framework that can adaptively learn and transfer appropriate dependency structures conditioned on context is needed to improve predictive accuracy and interpretability in clinical and other multi-fidelity systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework that conditions the structure learning process of a multi-output graphical model on local input features will yield a more expressive and functionally relevant family of covariance functions, leading to superior predictive performance in tasks with heterogeneous output relationships compared to static multi-output kernels.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural meta-model that takes a task descriptor or local input features and outputs parameters governing a structure learning process, such as the sparsity penalty in a graphical lasso applied to a base kernel's precision matrix. This will be integrated into a multi-output Gaussian process framework, building on the Emukit toolkit for decision-making under uncertainty. The entire system—meta-model, graphical structure learner, and GP hyperparameters—will be trained end-to-end via variational inference, optimizing the marginal likelihood on tasks like longitudinal biomarker prediction from the PIMS-TS domain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled method for constructing context-aware multi-output kernels, advancing the state-of-the-art in flexible multi-task learning. It will deliver a scalable software framework within the Emukit ecosystem, enabling application to domains like clinical biomarker analysis and multi-fidelity simulation where relationships between outputs are non-stationary.
proposals.novelty: While multi-output GPs and graphical models for kernels exist, the key innovation is the meta-learned, context-conditional generation of the dependency structure itself, allowing the kernel's implicit graphical model to vary smoothly across the input space, which is a departure from prior static or discrete mixture approaches.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent Task Structure with Bayesian Nonparametric Niche Models: A Framework for Multi-Fidelity and Multi-Output Learning
proposals.motivation: In clinical biomarker studies and multi-fidelity simulation, data often arise from an unknown number of latent processes or tasks, such as distinct patient subgroups or simulation fidelities. Pre-specifying this structure is difficult and can bias models. Current multi-task and multi-output Gaussian processes struggle with unknown, potentially infinite task cardinality, limiting their flexibility in real-world systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hierarchical Bayesian nonparametric prior, analogous to the Chinese Restaurant Process used in ecological niche modeling, can effectively infer the latent task structure from observational data, leading to more accurate and interpretable models of output correlations in multi-fidelity and longitudinal clinical settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical model where each data point is assigned to a latent task via a CRP prior, allowing an unbounded number of tasks. Conditional on assignments, outputs are modeled with task-specific Gaussian processes or linear mixed models. Inference will use MCMC or variational methods, building on the Emukit toolkit for decision-making under uncertainty. The framework will be validated on real-world longitudinal biomarker data from children (e.g., PIMS-TS) and multi-fidelity ice-sheet simulation outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven method to discover latent task structure, enhancing multi-output prediction. It contributes a novel modeling framework to Neil Lawrence's research themes, bridging ecological analogy and clinical/multi-fidelity systems. The resulting software will extend Emukit for flexible, nonparametric task modeling.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of ecological co-occurrence models to latent task discovery in machine learning is novel. Unlike standard multi-task learning, our approach does not require the number of tasks to be known a priori, offering a fundamentally more flexible 'between'-data modeling framework.

proposals.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Dynamic Fault Trees for Adaptive Safety Assurance in Clinical Machine Learning Pipelines
proposals.motivation: The deployment of ML systems in high-stakes domains like healthcare requires rigorous safety assurance. Traditional reliability engineering tools, like static fault trees, are ill-equipped to handle the unbounded, evolving space of potential ML failures arising from data drift, adversarial inputs, or unforeseen interactions. This gap creates significant risk, especially for longitudinal applications like biomarker monitoring in pediatric patients (e.g., PIMS-TS), where failure modes are not fully enumerable a priori.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian nonparametric prior, placed over a latent space of failure modes, can model their emergence and complex correlations within an ML pipeline, and that integrating this model into a dynamic fault tree framework will yield a more accurate, adaptive quantification of system risk compared to static analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework using Indian Buffet Process (IBP) or Beta-Bernoulli process priors to model an unbounded set of latent failure features. These features will be linked, via a probabilistic graphical model, to observable indicators in the ML pipeline (e.g., shifts in predictive entropy, biomarker prediction errors). This model will be integrated into a dynamic fault tree, implemented and extended within the Emukit toolkit for decision-making under uncertainty, allowing for real-time risk propagation and updating as new 'failure features' are inferred from operational data.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, mathematically grounded framework for reliability analysis of ML systems that accounts for unknown-unknowns. 2) A practical, open-source software tool extending Emukit for adaptive safety assessment. 3) A case study applying the method to a real-world clinical ML pipeline for longitudinal biomarker analysis, demonstrating its utility for system design and hardening.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of Bayesian nonparametrics with dynamic fault trees for ML safety is novel, moving beyond static risk registers. It directly addresses the 'between' theme—modelling the latent space between enumerated component failures and emergent system-level faults—which static analyses cannot capture.

proposals.title: Sparse Multi-Output Kernels via Dictionary Learning: A Compressed Sensing Framework for Interpretable Multi-Fidelity and Longitudinal Modelling
proposals.motivation: Operator-valued kernels (OVKs) are powerful for multi-output tasks like multi-fidelity simulation and longitudinal biomarker analysis, but they are often computationally expensive and lack interpretability. Designing a parsimonious OVK that captures the essential structure between outputs—such as the relationship between cheap and expensive simulations or between biomarkers over time—remains a significant challenge. This limits their practical utility in complex, data-scarce domains like healthcare and climate modelling where understanding inter-output relationships is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a complex operator-valued kernel can be represented as a sparse combination of simple, interpretable base kernels, and that this sparse representation can be efficiently recovered using optimization techniques analogous to compressed sensing, leading to more interpretable and computationally efficient models.
proposals.method: We will formulate kernel learning as a sparse dictionary recovery problem. A dictionary of simple OVKs (e.g., separable, diagonal, block-diagonal) will be constructed or learned. Using the Emukit framework for decision-making under uncertainty, we will set up an inverse problem where the 'measurements' are empirical covariance estimates from multi-fidelity or longitudinal patient data. We will then solve for the sparse coefficients using group Lasso regularization, tailored to the structure of operator-valued atoms, promoting a minimal set of fundamental inter-task interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for learning compact, interpretable multi-output models, directly enhancing tasks like biomarker trajectory analysis and multi-fidelity experimental design. It will contribute a new theoretical and practical bridge between compressed sensing and kernel methods, implemented within an open-source toolkit (extending Emukit) for broader use in systems and biomedical research.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious adaptation lies in rigorously mapping the compressed sensing paradigm—where sparsity is in a transform domain—to the functional domain of operator-valued kernels, and in developing the corresponding structured sparsity penalties for operator-valued dictionary atoms, which is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Federated Multi-Output Gaussian Processes via Consensus ADMM for Privacy-Preserving Biomarker Modeling
proposals.motivation: Multi-output Gaussian processes (MOGPs) are powerful for modeling correlated outputs, such as longitudinal biomarker panels in pediatric patients, but scaling them to large, distributed datasets is a major challenge. In healthcare and other sensitive domains, data is often siloed across institutions due to privacy and governance constraints, preventing the centralization required for standard MOGP training. This creates a critical need for federated learning methods that can build unified, high-fidelity models without sharing raw data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the consensus optimization paradigm, specifically the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM), can be adapted to enable communication-efficient, federated training of MOGPs, allowing disparate data holders to collaboratively learn a global model's hyperparameters and coregionalization structure while keeping data local.
proposals.method: We will develop a federated MOGP framework where each client (e.g., a hospital) holds a local partition of the multi-output data. Each client maintains a local MOGP surrogate, defined by shared global hyperparameters (kernel parameters and coregionalization matrix B) and its local data. The global objective combines local log-likelihoods with an l2 consensus penalty on the hyperparameters. We will employ ADMM to iteratively optimize this: clients perform local updates in parallel, then communicate their hyperparameter estimates to a coordinator (or via peer-to-peer averaging) to form a consensus update, iterating until convergence. The Emukit toolkit will provide the foundation for probabilistic modeling and decision-making under uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first federated learning algorithm specifically designed for multi-output Gaussian processes, enabling large-scale, privacy-aware modeling of correlated systems. It will provide a practical software framework for collaborative research on sensitive, multi-fidelity datasets, directly applicable to problems like distributed biomarker analysis and multi-site clinical studies.
proposals.novelty: While consensus ADMM is established in distributed optimization and single-output GPs have been federated, its application to the structurally complex problem of MOGPs—where both kernel parameters and the inter-output correlation structure must be consensualized—is novel. This bridges the 'between' of distributed systems theory and probabilistic multi-output modeling.

proposals.title: Just-in-Time Federated Learning: A Supply-Chain-Inspired Framework for Multi-Output Gaussian Processes
proposals.motivation: Federated learning for complex models like multi-output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) faces prohibitive communication and synchronization costs, especially with heterogeneous, longitudinal data common in healthcare (e.g., biomarker trajectories across patients). Current methods treat all data shards and updates equally, leading to computational waste and inefficiency, mirroring unoptimized logistics networks. This project addresses the critical need for resource-aware, adaptive distributed learning that respects data provenance and variable client utility, a gap between federated optimization and principled uncertainty modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling federated MOGP training as a supply-chain network—where data updates are inventory, clients are suppliers, and the central model is a distribution hub—we can develop optimization algorithms that dynamically prioritize high-value information flows. This will yield more communication- and compute-efficient training without sacrificing model fidelity, effectively balancing predictive accuracy with system costs.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit framework to a federated setting, integrating concepts from inventory control (e.g., Economic Order Quantity models) and logistics. Client-side MOGP updates (partial kernel matrices, hyperparameter gradients) will be treated as items with associated 'holding costs' (local compute) and 'stockout costs' (global model error). A central coordinator, using multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization techniques akin to prior ice-sheet work, will dynamically request updates based on predicted information gain and network latency, implementing a just-in-time update protocol. This will be validated on synthetic data and real-world, partitioned longitudinal biomarker datasets from pediatric studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled software framework for resource-efficient federated MOGPs, explicitly trading off model accuracy with system costs. It will deliver new algorithms for adaptive client selection and update scheduling, grounded in operations research. The resulting methodology will enable the application of sophisticated correlated-output models to previously intractable distributed data problems in healthcare and beyond.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal fusion of supply-chain management models with federated Bayesian learning, creating a non-obvious cross-disciplinary optimization lens. While federated learning and MOGPs exist, no pre-2026 work systematically applies inventory and logistics theory to manage the provenance, quality, and flow of uncertain model updates in a distributed system.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Ambiguity-Aware Framework for Input Inference in Multi-Output Gaussian Processes with Applications to Biomarker Trajectory Analysis
proposals.motivation: Inverting a kernel mapping to infer inputs from observed outputs is a fundamental ill-posed problem with critical applications in systems biology and personalized medicine. For instance, understanding the latent physiological state (input) driving a patient's longitudinal biomarker profile (output) is essential for diagnosis and treatment, but many distinct states can map to similar observations. Pre-2026 methods often provide single point estimates without quantifying this inherent ambiguity, leading to potentially misleading conclusions in clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by placing a structured prior over the input space and performing full Bayesian inference with a multi-output Gaussian process model, we can rigorously characterize the posterior distribution over possible inputs, explicitly identifying recoverable features and directions of high ambiguity.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework within the Emukit software ecosystem. The method combines a known multi-output kernel (e.g., for correlated biomarker trajectories) with an informative prior over inputs (e.g., a Gaussian process prior or one informed by population-level data). We will perform approximate posterior inference using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo or variational inference, leveraging recent advances in decision-making under uncertainty. The posterior geometry will be analyzed via its covariance to identify principal directions of high variance (ambiguity) and low variance (identifiable features).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, uncertainty-quantifying tool for the ill-posed inverse problem in kernel methods, moving beyond point estimates. It will deliver a practical software implementation for researchers and clinicians, enabling them to distinguish confident inferences from ambiguous ones in areas like biomarker interpretation and experimental design.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian inference in GPs is standard, its application to rigorously solve and diagnose the *inverse* problem of input inference, with explicit focus on quantifying and interpreting ambiguity in the input space, is novel. This bridges Neil Lawrence's themes of 'between' (connecting states to observations) and 'design' (for decision-making) in a unified framework.

proposals.title: Federated Inverse Inference for Multi-Output Gaussian Processes: A Consensus-Based Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: Inverse problems in spatial statistics, such as sensor calibration and source localization, are increasingly constrained by data fragmentation across institutions due to privacy (e.g., medical biomarker data) and communication limits (e.g., sensor networks). Centralizing data for inference with multi-output Gaussian processes (MOGPs) is often infeasible, yet existing distributed GP methods focus on forward prediction, not the inverse problem of recovering hidden inputs from observed outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated optimization framework, which performs distributed inverse inference by having clients compute local posterior summaries over the shared input space and iteratively reach consensus, can accurately recover hidden inputs without sharing raw data, scaling to massive, partitioned output datasets.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit framework to implement a federated variational inference scheme. Each client holds a partition of the multi-output observations and locally approximates the posterior over the latent inputs using a sparse variational MOGP. Clients will then share only the parameters of their local variational distributions (or gradients thereof) with a central coordinator, which will aggregate them using a consensus ADMM or distributed stochastic gradient descent algorithm to update a global estimate of the hidden input field, iterating until convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled algorithm for distributed inverse inference with GPs, enabling large-scale, privacy-preserving applications in federated sensor calibration and medical imaging. It will contribute a new module to the Emukit toolkit for decision-making under distributed data constraints.
proposals.novelty: The approach novelly combines federated optimization with the inverse problem for multi-output GPs, moving beyond distributed forward modeling. It addresses the 'between' systems of data ownership by designing communication protocols that exchange inferences, not data, aligning with privacy-aware biomarker research.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent Patient Trajectories from Multi-Output Biomarker Kernels: A Probabilistic Inverse Framework for Clinical Phenotyping
proposals.motivation: In clinical biomarker studies, such as longitudinal analysis of inflammatory markers in children with PIMS-TS, we often model observed multi-output biomarker data with Gaussian processes using structured multi-output kernels that encode physiological relationships. However, the inverse problem—recovering the latent patient-specific inputs (e.g., a low-dimensional phenotypic state) that generated the observed outputs given a known kernel—remains unaddressed. Solving this is crucial for moving from descriptive modeling to mechanistic interpretation and personalized patient stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, for a known multi-output kernel derived from domain knowledge (e.g., a linear model of coregionalization), the latent inputs corresponding to observed function values can be probabilistically inferred, providing a novel, interpretable representation of individual patients within a cohort.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inversion framework within the Emukit toolkit. The core algorithm will treat the known multi-output kernel as a fixed forward model and place a prior over the latent input space. Using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (via PyMC3 or GPflow) and variational inference, we will sample from the posterior distribution of latent inputs given observed, potentially noisy, multi-output function evaluations. The framework will be tested on synthetic data and applied to real-world longitudinal biomarker datasets from pediatric inflammatory disease studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, open-source method (extending Emukit) for solving a foundational inverse problem in kernel methods. It will deliver a new tool for clinical researchers to infer interpretable, low-dimensional patient states from complex longitudinal biomarker data, bridging the gap between flexible kernel models and actionable clinical insights.
proposals.novelty: While kernel design and forward prediction are well-studied, the systematic probabilistic inversion for recovering inputs from multi-output kernels is novel. This directly inverts the standard GP paradigm, moving from 'kernel + inputs -> outputs' to 'kernel + outputs -> inputs', which is non-trivial for structured, multi-output settings.

proposals.title: Operationalizing Multi-Output Gaussian Processes: A DevOps Framework for Continuous Kernel Lifecycle Management
proposals.motivation: Multi-output Gaussian processes (MOGPs) with operator-valued kernels are powerful for modeling correlated, high-dimensional outputs, such as longitudinal biomarker trajectories in pediatric patients. However, deploying them in real-world, data-streaming settings like clinical monitoring is hindered by the static nature of model design and the computational complexity of kernel operations. Current low-rank approximations fix structure a priori, creating a brittle gap between scalable model development and reliable, efficient operation in production systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating the low-rank structure and hyperparameters of an operator-valued kernel as mutable operational artifacts within a DevOps-integrated system, we can create a self-optimizing MOGP framework that automatically balances accuracy and efficiency, enabling robust continuous deployment in dynamic environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit toolkit to implement a DevOps loop for MOGPs. The framework will use inducing point methods and structured kernel interpolation (SKI) for low-rank approximations, treating the inducing set and interpolation grid as versioned artifacts. Continuous integration pipelines will test new kernel configurations against performance regressions using multi-fidelity simulation (inspired by ice-sheet work). A/B testing and canary deployments will manage updates, while real-time monitoring of predictive entropy and computational latency will trigger automated rollbacks or adaptations of model complexity via the hyperparameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel software framework that unifies the design and operation of scalable multi-output models, directly applicable to longitudinal clinical data analysis. It will advance the field of decision-making under uncertainty by introducing principles of continuous lifecycle management to probabilistic model cores, enhancing both robustness and practical utility.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in applying DevOps practices—versioning, CI/CD, canary deployment—to the intrinsic mathematical components of a probabilistic model (the kernel), creating a feedback loop where operational metrics directly govern model structure evolution.

proposals.title: A Controlled Trial Framework for Causal Evaluation of Machine Learning Robustness to Real-World Data Quality Pathologies
proposals.motivation: Machine learning systems often fail silently when deployed due to unanticipated data quality issues like missingness, label noise, and distribution shift, which are not captured by static test sets. This is especially critical in domains like healthcare, where Neil Lawrence's work on biomarkers and longitudinal modeling highlights the gap between curated research data and messy, real-world clinical streams. A principled methodology to causally attribute performance degradation to specific data pathologies is needed to build trustworthy, adaptive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a phased, controlled trial protocol, where data quality degradations are introduced as explicit interventions, can generate rigorous causal evidence for which model architectures and training regimens are robust to specific, measurable real-world data failures.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-phase framework using Emukit for decision-making under uncertainty to manage the trial. In Phase I ('simulation'), multi-fidelity Gaussian processes will model performance as a function of data pathology severity. In Phase II ('controlled exposure'), a 'treatment' model group will be exposed to a real-world data stream with known, quantified pathologies (e.g., MNAR missingness, concept drift), while a 'control' group receives repaired data. We will employ longitudinal multilevel models, as used in Lawrence's biomarker research, to analyze the performance trajectories and isolate the causal effect of each pathology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for the pre-deployment stress-testing of ML systems, moving beyond correlation to causal understanding of failure modes. It will yield a software toolkit for designing and analyzing such trials, contributing directly to safer, evidence-based deployment in non-stationary environments like digital health.
proposals.novelty: The approach transposes the formal structure of clinical trials—with pre-registered analysis plans and controlled interventions—to the evaluation of ML system robustness, treating data quality issues not as noise but as the central variable under study.

proposals.title: Between Systems and Patients: A Decision-Theoretic Framework for Continuous Biomarker Model Lifecycle Orchestration
proposals.motivation: The deployment of machine learning models for clinical biomarkers, as in pediatric inflammatory conditions like PIMS-TS, is hampered by a rigid separation between development and operations. This creates latency in responding to data drift from new patient cohorts and friction in validating updated models, hindering the agile, evidence-based evolution required for real-world clinical utility. A unified approach is needed to close the loop between monitoring, experimental retraining, and robust deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: That a unified 'ML DevOp' role, supported by an integrated, policy-driven software framework for automated lifecycle decisions, will produce more agile, accountable, and empirically-grounded model evolution in clinical biomarker applications than traditional siloed workflows.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel software framework extending principles from Emukit for decision-making under uncertainty. The core is a policy engine that uses multi-fidelity experimental design to plan cost-efficient retraining experiments triggered by monitoring alerts (e.g., biomarker prediction drift). The framework will automate the construction of longitudinal multilevel models to assess candidate models, and integrate Bayesian optimization for A/B testing orchestration, creating a closed-loop system for a single practitioner to manage.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete, open-source framework for continuous ML that operationalizes the DevOp paradigm in clinical ML settings. It will provide a methodology for embedding operational intelligence and decision-theoretic experimental design directly into the model lifecycle, reducing handoff friction and enabling systematic, data-driven model updates.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing decision-theoretic experimental design (multi-fidelity, Bayesian optimization) with longitudinal clinical data modeling into a unified orchestration framework, explicitly designed to empower a single practitioner role rather than separate teams, which is unexplored in pre-2026 MLops research.

proposals.title: A Regulatory-Adaptive Kernel Framework for Multi-Output Biomarker Trajectory Modelling
proposals.motivation: In healthcare, multi-output models for patient biomarkers must capture complex, often asymmetric relationships (e.g., regulatory feedback, causal pathways) between physiological signals. Simultaneously, these models operate under stringent, evolving legal and ethical constraints (e.g., GDPR, AI Act). Current Gaussian process frameworks are limited by symmetric, positive-definite kernels and offer no formal mechanism for encoding dynamic regulatory rules as model priors, risking non-compliance.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multi-output learning framework, using a kernel whose structure is governed by a machine-readable regulatory specification, can effectively model asymmetric biomarker relationships while ensuring the model's inferences automatically adapt to remain within evolving legal and ethical bounds.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit framework to implement a multi-output Gaussian process with a non-standard, potentially indefinite kernel matrix, parameterized to encode directed relationships. A formal constraint language (e.g., based on declarative logic) will define permissible model behaviors from regulatory texts. This specification will act as a regularizer within the model's objective function, directly shaping the kernel's parameters during optimization. We will validate the framework using longitudinal, multilevel biomarker data from pediatric inflammatory syndromes (PIMS-TS), where regulatory constraints on data use and prediction are critical.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new software framework for compliant, multi-fidelity modelling in sensitive domains. It will deliver a principled method for integrating hard, external constraints into kernel design, advancing the field of responsible AI. The resulting models will be inherently auditable, as the kernel structure directly reflects the active regulatory rules.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a flexible, asymmetric relational kernel with a dynamic, machine-readable regulatory layer is novel. It moves beyond static fairness constraints to a system where the core learning prior is continuously shaped by a formal representation of the external governance environment.

proposals.title: Deconfounding Model Lifecycles: A Causal Framework for Joint Retraining and Experimentation
proposals.motivation: In production machine learning systems, continuous retraining is essential to maintain performance, but its impact is confounded by natural temporal drift in the data-generating process. Standard A/B tests cannot disentangle whether observed changes in key performance indicators are due to the model update or external trends, leading to invalid scientific conclusions and poor deployment decisions. This problem is acute in domains like digital health, where biomarker trajectories in patients and children are influenced by both treatment and underlying disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally modeling the retraining policy, data drift, and observed outcomes within a causal graphical model, we can deconfound the effect of model updates from temporal drift, enabling valid counterfactual comparisons and statistically robust assessment of retraining interventions.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian nonparametric framework, extending concepts from multi-fidelity modeling and decision-making under uncertainty (as in Emukit), to treat retraining events as interventions in a temporal causal graph. The system will integrate longitudinal multilevel modeling (as used for biomarker analysis) to separate within-system variation from policy effects. We will employ Gaussian process state-space models to capture latent temporal drift and develop novel experimental design algorithms that optimize the joint policy of when to retrain and when to A/B test.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, unified statistical framework for lifecycle management of deployed models, delivering formal guarantees for causal effect estimation of retraining. It will yield open-source software (extending the Emukit philosophy) for designing and analyzing continuous experimentation systems, directly applicable to real-world data challenges in healthcare and beyond.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference and continuous experimentation are studied separately, their integration for model lifecycle management is novel. The key innovation is treating the retraining scheduler and the A/B test designer as a single, optimizable system whose components are formally deconfounded through causal identification.

proposals.title: A Federated, Uncertainty-Aware Framework for Continuous Biomarker Modeling with Production Safeguards
proposals.motivation: Modern healthcare and scientific studies, such as longitudinal biomarker analysis in pediatric syndromes like PIMS-TS, generate sensitive, multi-output data distributed across institutions. While Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) are ideal for modeling correlated outputs with uncertainty, their training traditionally requires centralized data, violating privacy. Furthermore, continuously updating such models on live, federated data streams risks performance regression if new versions are deployed without rigorous validation against production baselines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating federated optimization for MOGPs with a principled, multi-fidelity A/B testing protocol, we can create a system that enables continuous, safe improvement of complex probabilistic models on distributed data without centralization or catastrophic performance loss.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit toolkit to support federated, variational inference for MOGPs (e.g., using the Linear Model of Coregionalization), enabling collaborative training across partitioned patient datasets. A central coordinator will orchestrate distributed optimization rounds, aggregating only model updates. New model versions will enter a production A/B testing framework, where their predictive performance and uncertainty calibration are statistically compared against the incumbent model on held-out validation tasks, using sequential testing methods to manage risk, before a deployment decision is made.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source software framework for continuous federated learning of expressive probabilistic models, bridging the gap between advanced statistical modeling and real-world ML operations. It will provide a methodological blueprint for safely evolving models in privacy-sensitive domains like healthcare, contributing to both the machine learning and clinical research communities.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of federated MOGP training with production-grade A/B testing for model validation is novel, moving beyond one-time federated learning to a continuous, risk-managed lifecycle for complex, correlated-output models.

proposals.title: Dynamic Data-Environment Cards: A Framework for Auditable Model Versioning in Non-Stationary Clinical Deployments
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models deployed in real-world settings, such as clinical biomarker monitoring, often degrade due to unrecorded shifts in the data environment, including changes in measurement protocols, patient demographics, or sensor noise. Existing model cards are static and lack the granular, versioned data provenance needed to diagnose this performance drift. This creates a critical gap in accountable AI systems, especially for longitudinal patient data where understanding the 'between' context of different deployment phases is essential for trust and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: That augmenting model versioning with continuous, quantified documentation of the live data environment will create a causal, auditable trace for performance drift, enabling more robust and interpretable triggers for model adaptation than monitoring performance metrics alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit toolkit to implement a Bayesian framework for online data-environment profiling, tracking key indicators (e.g., cohort summary statistics, missingness patterns, biomarker covariance shifts) as time-varying Gaussian processes. Each model inference will be logged with a vector of these profile features, creating a paired (model, data-context) version. Change-point detection and multilevel modeling (inspired by Lawrence's work on longitudinal biomarker responses) will analyze this trace to distinguish data-quality drift from concept drift, triggering adaptation protocols defined in a structured 'Data-Environment Card'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source framework for data-centric model governance, moving beyond static documentation. It will contribute a formal methodology for linking model performance directly to quantified data environment states, which is crucial for regulatory science and safe deployment in non-stationary real-world systems like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While model cards and performance monitoring exist, the integration of continuous, versioned data-environment profiling directly into the model deployment ledger—treating the data stream's statistical properties as a first-class, auditable entity—is a novel fusion of statistical process control and ML documentation.

proposals.title: A Clinical Trial Paradigm for Multi-Task Learning: Phased Validation with Non-Stationary Correlation Kernels
proposals.motivation: Validating machine learning models for real-world deployment, especially in clinical or biomarker settings, requires assessing robustness to distributional shifts over time. Standard static validation fails to detect insidious failures like gradual correlation shifts between related tasks, analogous to a drug's safety profile changing post-market. A principled, dynamic validation framework is urgently needed to certify models for non-stationary systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating a multi-task model as an intervention in a phased validation trial, and explicitly modeling the evolution of inter-task correlations with non-stationary kernels, will formally identify failure modes (e.g., catastrophic forgetting, correlation shift) that are invisible to standard evaluation, providing a dynamic certificate of robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a multi-task Gaussian process (GP) or deep neural network is evaluated across sequential validation phases. The core innovation is the integration of non-stationary inter-task covariance kernels (e.g., based on spectral mixture or neural network-input kernels) within a multi-fidelity or multi-output GP model. Using tools from Emukit, we will design the validation phases and statistically test for significant changes in the kernel parameters over time, treating these changes as primary validation metrics. The protocol will be tested on longitudinal biomarker data from pediatric cohorts (PIMS-TS) and multi-fidelity simulation outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel validation methodology that bridges clinical trial design and machine learning, delivering a formal protocol for dynamic model assessment. It will contribute new, interpretable metrics for model robustness in non-stationary environments and extend the Emukit software with modules for phased, multi-task validation.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of phased trial methodology with non-stationary multi-task learning for validation is novel. Existing multi-task GPs assume stationary correlations; here, non-stationarity in the task relationships is the central object of study and the key indicator of model failure.

proposals.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Supply Networks for Adaptive Management of Multi-Fidelity Data Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning pipelines, especially in domains like healthcare biomarker analysis, are complex data supply chains where intermediate artifacts degrade and errors propagate, impacting downstream decisions. Current pipeline tools lack principled statistical models to infer the latent, evolving dependencies between data quality, provenance events, and model performance. This creates brittle systems that cannot proactively manage computational resources or forecast integrity risks.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling an ML pipeline as a supply network with Bayesian nonparametric priors over its latent failure modes and dependency structures, we can create a system that automatically infers complex correlation patterns and enables adaptive, resource-efficient management of data integrity.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where each pipeline stage (data source, transformation, model) is a 'node' with inventory (artifacts) and logistics (dependencies). We will place a hierarchical Dirichlet process (HDP) prior over the unknown number of latent 'failure regimes' and use Gaussian process (GP) state-space models, building on Emukit, to capture quality degradation and propagation. Inference will use variational methods to learn these structures from pipeline execution traces, enabling real-time predictions of bottlenecks and recommendations for rerouting or quality control.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, statistically grounded framework for adaptive data pipeline management that moves beyond static rules. It will provide new methodology for modeling uncertainty in complex, evolving computational systems and deliver open-source software integrating nonparametric Bayesian models with pipeline orchestration.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of supply chain inventory theory with Bayesian nonparametrics for data pipeline management is novel. Unlike prior work on workflow provenance or static quality checks, this approach infers an unbounded number of latent statistical 'tasks' governing integrity, enabling truly adaptive behavior.

proposals.title: Designing Non-Stationary Deep Gaussian Processes for Multi-Fidelity Biomarker Trajectory Modeling
proposals.motivation: In clinical biomarker studies, such as longitudinal analysis of inflammatory markers in children with PIMS-TS, the underlying dynamics are inherently non-stationary, with variability changing over time and across patient subgroups. Standard Gaussian Process (GP) models, with stationary kernels, fail to capture these complex, input-dependent variations, limiting their utility for personalized prediction and decision-making under uncertainty in healthcare systems. This gap is critical for designing effective monitoring frameworks that operate between population-level trends and individual patient trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating explicit, input-dependent non-stationary kernels into the layers of a Deep Gaussian Process (Deep GP) will significantly improve the model's capacity to capture heterogeneous, multi-scale dynamics in longitudinal biomarker data and multi-fidelity simulation outputs, compared to stationary Deep GPs or shallow non-stationary models.
proposals.method: We will extend the Deep GP framework, building upon software like Emukit, by implementing input-dependent warpings or spectral mixture kernels within each layer to induce non-stationarity. The architecture will be tailored for multi-fidelity settings (linking low-fidelity population models to high-fidelity individual patient data) and for longitudinal modeling via multilevel designs. Inference will leverage scalable variational methods suitable for different data modalities. The model will be validated on real-world biomarker datasets and applied to a multi-fidelity ice-sheet simulation case study to demonstrate generality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled, flexible modeling framework for non-stationary, multi-fidelity systems, directly applicable to clinical biomarker analysis and computational simulation design. It will provide new software tools within the Emukit ecosystem for decision-making under uncertainty with complex data. The research will yield methodological insights into the design of deep probabilistic models that better reflect the 'between' nature of real-world processes.
proposals.novelty: While non-stationary kernels exist for shallow GPs and Deep GPs have been explored, their combination for designing deep, structured non-stationarity—particularly within a multi-fidelity or longitudinal multilevel framework—is novel. This moves beyond simply adding capacity to explicitly designing inductive biases for input-dependent dynamics across layers.

proposals.title: Deep Hierarchical Variational Inference for Multi-Fidelity Biomarker Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In complex systems like pediatric inflammatory syndromes (e.g., PIMS-TS), biomarker responses are influenced by a deep hierarchy of factors, from cellular pathways to patient-specific physiology and treatment regimens. Current variational inference (VI) methods, as implemented in toolkits like Emukit, are typically validated on shallow hierarchies (<5 layers), limiting their ability to capture these intricate, multi-scale dependencies for robust uncertainty quantification and decision-making in clinical and experimental design settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the variational framework to deeper, structured hierarchies, we can achieve more scalable and accurate probabilistic models of complex, multi-fidelity systems, leading to improved uncertainty estimates for downstream tasks like biomarker trajectory prediction and experimental design.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework for deep hierarchical VI by extending the multilevel modeling approach used for longitudinal biomarker data to architectures with >5 latent layers. The core technical innovation will be the design of structured, amortized variational families that respect the system's causal and fidelity structure, implemented within the Emukit software framework. We will test scalability using stochastic variational inference and evaluate on two pre-2026 case studies: 1) Simulating ice-sheet dynamics (a multi-fidelity physical system) and 2) Modeling the longitudinal response of inflammatory biomarkers in children with PIMS-TS to anti-inflammatory drugs, leveraging real-world EHR data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable, principled framework for deep probabilistic modeling in systems with complex hierarchies, directly applicable to clinical biomarker analysis and simulation-based science. It will extend the methodological reach of toolkits like Emukit and provide a concrete benchmark for the performance of deep VI in real-world, multi-fidelity contexts.
proposals.novelty: While deep neural networks are common, the principled extension of *variational Bayesian inference* to deep (>5 layer) hierarchical models, particularly for blending mechanistic multi-fidelity simulators with noisy real-world patient data, remains an open and non-trivial challenge at the intersection of statistics and machine learning.

proposals.title: Scaling Deep Gaussian Processes for Complex Clinical Phenotypes: A Multi-Fidelity Framework for Longitudinal Biomarker Analysis
proposals.motivation: Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) offer a principled framework for modeling complex, non-stationary relationships, but their application has been largely confined to small-scale synthetic or benchmark datasets (e.g., small digit sets). In clinical biomarker research, particularly for complex pediatric inflammatory syndromes like PIMS-TS, data is high-dimensional, longitudinal, and multi-faceted, creating a critical gap between advanced probabilistic models and real-world clinical utility. Scaling DGPs to such complex, structured datasets would unlock their potential for robust uncertainty quantification in high-stakes decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, multi-fidelity DGP architecture, informed by domain knowledge of biomarker dynamics, can be scaled to model large, complex longitudinal clinical datasets, providing superior predictive performance and more interpretable uncertainty estimates compared to standard GP or deep neural network approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the Emukit toolkit to implement a novel DGP framework designed for longitudinal data. The architecture will use inducing point approximations and inter-domain variational inference for scalability. Crucially, we will incorporate a multi-fidelity design, inspired by Lawrence's work on ice-sheet simulation, to integrate different data sources (e.g., sparse high-precision lab measurements with dense, noisy wearable sensor data). The model will be tested on a large-scale, real-world longitudinal biomarker dataset from pediatric patients with inflammatory conditions, building on prior multilevel modeling work.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, open-source DGP implementation within Emukit for complex clinical data. It will provide a methodological advance in applying deep probabilistic models to large, structured real-world datasets. The resulting framework will offer clinicians a tool for predicting individual patient biomarker trajectories with quantified uncertainty, directly supporting personalized treatment decisions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of a scalable DGP architecture with a multi-fidelity data model specifically tailored for the complexities of longitudinal clinical biomarker data, moving beyond small-scale proofs-of-concept to address a pressing real-world modeling challenge.

proposals.title: A Multi-Fidelity, Systems-Based Framework for Efficient Optimization of Deep Gaussian Processes
proposals.motivation: Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) offer a powerful, Bayesian framework for modeling complex, hierarchical data, such as longitudinal biomarker trajectories in pediatric patients. However, their adoption in real-world clinical and scientific settings is hampered by the computational cost and instability of optimizing the variational lower bound, especially with complex likelihoods. More efficient and robust optimization is needed to bridge the gap between these flexible models and practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging multi-fidelity optimization principles and a systems-level re-parameterization of the variational objective, we can develop a training framework that significantly accelerates convergence and improves robustness for deep GPs, particularly in data-scarce or multi-task scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel optimization framework within the Emukit software ecosystem. The approach will treat the DGP's variational bound as a system of interacting components, applying multi-fidelity ideas where cheaper, approximate evaluations (e.g., on subsets of data or with simplified posteriors) guide optimization of the full objective. We will specifically design adaptive learning rate schedules and natural gradient updates tailored to the structure of the deep variational distribution, moving beyond generic stochastic gradient descent.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, open-source software tool for robust DGP training, directly enabling their use in demanding applications like longitudinal biomarker analysis. It will contribute a new methodological perspective by framing DGP optimization as a multi-fidelity systems design problem.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing concepts from experimental design (multi-fidelity) and systems modeling into the core optimization loop for DGPs, an approach distinct from incremental improvements to variational approximations or gradient estimators prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Scalable and Accurate Posterior Inference in Deep Gaussian Processes
proposals.motivation: Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) offer a flexible, compositional framework for modeling complex data, particularly relevant in clinical biomarker studies and multi-fidelity systems where uncertainty quantification is critical. However, as of 2025, scalable and accurate posterior inference in DGPs remains a major challenge, with variational approximations often leading to biased uncertainty estimates that can compromise decision-making in sensitive applications like pediatric medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC), specifically the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), for posterior sampling in DGPs will yield more accurate representations of predictive uncertainty and model parameters compared to standard variational inference, without prohibitive computational cost when applied to structured, moderate-dimensional problems.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel inference framework within the Emukit software ecosystem, implementing HMC/NUTS sampling for DGP posteriors using automatic differentiation in PyTorch or TensorFlow Probability. The method will leverage the DGP's natural parameterization to define an efficient Hamiltonian system. We will employ multi-fidelity modeling techniques to warm-start chains from lower-fidelity variational approximations, and validate the approach on longitudinal biomarker datasets from pediatric inflammatory syndromes (e.g., PIMS-TS) and ice-sheet simulation calibration tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a robust, open-source tool for high-fidelity Bayesian inference in DGPs, directly enhancing the reliability of uncertainty estimates in clinical and scientific decision-making.
